title: What n8n Is — Visual Automation in Plain Language

content.explanation:
n8n (pronounced n-eight-n) is a visual workflow automation tool. Think of it as connecting different apps and services without writing code. You drag boxes (nodes) onto a canvas, draw lines between them, and data flows from one to the next.

What makes n8n different from Zapier or Make: it is open-source and self-hosted. Run it on your own server, your data never leaves your infrastructure, and no per-automation fees. Your automations scale with your server, not a subscription tier.

n8n has a visual editor in your browser. Build workflows by adding nodes, configuring them, connecting them. Activate and it runs automatically. Every trigger fires, the workflow executes all steps in order.

terminology.definition: An open-source, self-hosted workflow automation tool with a visual editor.
Software running on your own server. You control the data and infrastructure.
A sequence of automated steps that run in order. Data flows first to last.

codeExample.code:
# Install n8n globally
npm install -g n8n

# Start n8n (browser at http://localhost:5678)
n8n start

# Or run with Docker
docker run -it --rm --name n8n -p 5678:5678 n8nio/n8n
codeExample.annotation: Two ways to run n8n. npm for quick experiments, Docker for production.

title: n8n Core Concepts — Nodes, Workflows, Triggers

content.explanation:
Three concepts make up everything in n8n: nodes, workflows, and triggers. A node is a single action — send email, read spreadsheet, make HTTP request, transform data. Each takes data in, does something, passes data out.

A workflow chains nodes together. Data flows left to right. You can branch (if/else), merge branches, and loop. Simple as two nodes or complex as fifty.

Triggers are special nodes that start a workflow. They listen for events: webhook data, cron schedule, file change, new email. Without a trigger, a workflow just sits there. You can also trigger manually for testing.

terminology.definition: A single action in a workflow. Performs one task: read, transform, send, or decide.
A special node that starts a workflow when an event occurs.
One complete run of a workflow from trigger to final node.

codeExample.annotation: A 3-node workflow: trigger every hour, check API, email result. Basic pattern: trigger, process, output.

title: Calling Claude from n8n via HTTP Node

content.explanation:
The most direct way to use Claude in n8n: HTTP Request node calling the Anthropic Messages API. Configure it to POST to https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages with your API key in headers and prompt in the body.

You need three headers: x-api-key (your Anthropic key), anthropic-version (2023-06-01), and Content-Type: application/json. The response includes Claude text in content[0].text.

Store your API key as an n8n credential (Settings > Credentials), not hardcoded. Once working, feed any data from previous nodes into the prompt using n8n expressions like {{ $json.fieldName }}.

terminology.definition: An n8n node that makes web requests to any URL. Used to call the Anthropic API.
The REST endpoint for Claude at api.anthropic.com/v1/messages.
Syntax using {{ }} to reference data from previous nodes.

codeExample.annotation: HTTP Request calling Anthropic API. Prompt includes data from previous node via expression.

exercise.instruction: Set up n8n workflow with Manual Trigger + HTTP Request to Anthropic API. Send a simple prompt. Paste the response JSON.

validation.errorMessage: Paste the JSON response from Claude.

title: Claude as a Processing Node in a Workflow

content.explanation:
The real power: Claude is one step in a larger chain. Data comes from a form, spreadsheet, or API. Claude processes it (classifies, summarizes, extracts, rewrites). The result goes to email, database, or Slack. Claude becomes a smart processing node in the middle.

Example: customer feedback pipeline. Google Sheets trigger detects new review. Claude classifies as positive/negative/neutral, extracts topic, suggests response. A router sends positive reviews to testimonials, negative ones to support alerts.

Key technique: prompt templating. Instead of hardcoding prompts, inject data from previous nodes using n8n expressions. The prompt becomes a reusable template.

terminology.definition: A middle node that transforms data — receives input, processes intelligently, passes result.
A prompt with placeholder variables filled from real data at runtime.
An n8n node that sends data down different paths based on conditions.

codeExample.annotation: Three stages: Sheets trigger, Claude classifies, switch routes positive/negative differently.

content.explanation:
Build a complete workflow: receive a webhook with text, have Claude summarize it, send the summary back. Start with a Webhook node as trigger — creates a URL you can POST to. Connect to an HTTP Request node calling the Anthropic API. Add a Respond to Webhook node to return the summary.

Test by sending a POST request to your webhook URL with JSON body containing a text field. Watch data flow through each node in the execution view — raw input, Claude response, final output. If anything fails, n8n shows exactly which node errored.

terminology.definition: A URL that receives data via HTTP. In n8n, triggers the workflow when data arrives.
n8n debugging interface showing data at every step of a workflow run.
An n8n node that sends a response back to the webhook caller.

codeExample.annotation: Webhook receives text, Claude summarizes, response returns to caller.

exercise.instruction: Build the webhook workflow: receive text, send to Claude, return summary. Test with curl. Paste command and response.

validation.errorMessage: Paste curl command and Claude response.

title: Webhook Triggers — Events That Start Automations

content.explanation:
Webhooks are the most flexible trigger. A webhook creates a URL. Any external system sending data to that URL triggers your workflow. Connect n8n to anything making HTTP requests — forms, payment processors, CRMs, curl.

Common use: form submissions. Someone fills a contact form, data hits your n8n webhook. Claude qualifies the lead, composes a response, adds to CRM. All automatic from one form submission.

Beyond webhooks: Schedule triggers (cron), App triggers (new email, new Stripe payment, new Sheet row), and Polling triggers (periodic checks). Pick the trigger matching your "when."

terminology.definition: A unique URL generated by n8n that listens for incoming HTTP requests.
Fires at regular intervals based on cron expression or simple interval.
Periodically checks an external service for new data, unlike push-based webhooks.

codeExample.annotation: Four common triggers. Webhooks are most versatile. Schedules for time-based. App triggers for service events.

title: Having Claude Generate n8n Workflow JSON

content.explanation:
Claude can generate complete n8n workflow JSON from a natural language description. Instead of manually dragging nodes, describe what you want in plain English. Claude produces importable JSON you paste into n8n.

n8n workflows are stored as JSON — every node, connection, configuration. Claude translates descriptions like "when I get a Stripe payment, send a thank-you email" into complete workflow definitions.

Workflow: (1) describe to Claude, (2) Claude generates JSON, (3) import via n8n Import from JSON, (4) configure credentials Claude cannot know, (5) test and activate. Cuts build time from 30 minutes to 2 minutes.

terminology.definition: The JSON format n8n uses to store workflows. Contains nodes, configs, connections. Exportable/importable.
n8n feature to paste/upload a JSON workflow definition and create it instantly.
A spot where auth details need filling after import. Claude generates structure but not your secrets.

codeExample.code:
Prompt to Claude:
"Generate an n8n workflow JSON that does:
1. Triggers on webhook POST to /new-signup
2. Sends signup data to Claude for a welcome email
3. Sends the email via Gmail
4. Adds contact to Google Sheet

Output valid n8n workflow JSON I can import directly."

Claude generates complete JSON with all nodes and connections.
codeExample.annotation: Describe in English, Claude outputs importable JSON. You only add credentials — logic is done.

exercise.instruction: Ask Claude to generate n8n workflow JSON for: "Every day at 9 AM, fetch top 5 HN stories, summarize with Claude, email digest." Import into n8n. Confirm it worked.

validation.errorMessage: Paste your prompt and confirm import.

content.explanation:
Claude + n8n = describing automations in plain English and having them built. Not just generating JSON once — an ongoing workflow where you think in business terms and AI translates to technical implementation.

Three levels. Level 1: Claude generates workflow JSON, you import. Level 2: Claude explains nodes and helps debug. Level 3: Claude lives inside the workflow making runtime decisions (classifying, writing, extracting).

This shifts who can build automations. You do not need to memorize node libraries or API docs. Clearly describe what happens, when, and the output. Claude handles translation. Skill shifts from "configuring tools" to "knowing what to automate."

terminology.definition: Describing what an automation should do in plain English, AI generates the implementation.
AI inside a running workflow making decisions on live data — not just building, but processing.
Rules defining how your business operates. AI can implement these without code.

codeExample.code:
Business description:
"When someone fills my contact form:
1. Check if serious lead (budget > 00, specific project)
2. Serious: personalized email + Slack notification
3. Not serious: template response + newsletter signup"

Claude translates to 7 nodes:
Webhook -> Claude classifier -> Switch -> Branch A/B paths

Build time with Claude: ~5 minutes
Manual build time: ~45 minutes
codeExample.annotation: Real business automation in plain language. Claude designs the structure AND lives inside as classifier and email writer.

content.explanation:
Automations fail. APIs go down, rate limits hit, data arrives malformed. The difference between toy and production: error handling. n8n gives tools to catch errors, retry, and alert.

Every node can have an error workflow attached. When a node fails, the error routes to a separate workflow — Slack alert, log, or retry. For Claude API: most common errors are rate limits (429) and timeouts. Both fixable with retries.

n8n has built-in retry logic. Set 2-3 retries with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s). Add a timeout to HTTP Request nodes — if Claude takes over 30 seconds, fail and retry rather than hanging forever.

terminology.definition: A separate n8n workflow that runs when a node fails. For logging, alerting, or recovery.
Automatically re-running a failed node after a delay. Useful for temporary errors.
Retry strategy where waits increase: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s. Prevents hammering a struggling API.

codeExample.annotation: Retry config handles temporary failures. onError routes permanent failures to a separate path. Timeout prevents hangs.

title: Connecting n8n to Your Stack (Stripe, Google, Email)

content.explanation:
n8n has built-in nodes for hundreds of services: Stripe, Google Sheets, Gmail, Slack, Notion, Airtable, Mailchimp, Discord, Telegram. Each handles authentication and API details. Configure a node with credentials and use it like a building block.

Pattern for any service: (1) create credentials in n8n Settings, (2) add the service node, (3) select credential and configure, (4) connect to other nodes.

Common business pattern: Trigger (Stripe payment or webhook) then Claude processes then Output to multiple places (Sheets for records, Gmail for communication, Slack for notifications). This handles 80% of small business automations. The AI in the middle is what makes it intelligent.

terminology.definition: Authentication where you log into a service and grant n8n permission. More secure than API keys.
Stored auth info (API key, OAuth token) that n8n uses to connect to services. Encrypted, reusable.
Reusable structure for connecting services. Most common: Trigger then Process then Multi-output.

codeExample.annotation: Stripe triggers Claude for personalized email, Gmail sends, Sheets logs, Slack notifies. Five nodes, zero manual work.

title: Advanced: Multi-Step AI Chains in n8n

content.explanation:
Sometimes one Claude call is not enough. Complex tasks benefit from multiple AI steps, where each output feeds the next prompt. This is chaining, and n8n makes it visual — each Claude call is a separate node.

Example: support email processing. Step 1: extract facts (product, issue, urgency). Step 2: find solutions from knowledge base. Step 3: draft response using original email + solutions. Each gets a focused prompt, producing better results than one massive prompt.

Another pattern: generate-then-evaluate. Claude generates in one node, a second evaluates against guidelines. Failed checks loop back. This self-correcting chain produces higher quality than a single pass.

terminology.definition: Multiple AI calls in sequence, each output becoming next input. Breaks complex tasks into focused steps.
Two-step pattern: AI generates, second AI evaluates against criteria. Failed checks loop.
Breaking a complex prompt into multiple simpler prompts. Each handles one aspect. More reliable than mega-prompts.

codeExample.annotation: Three Claude calls: extract, solve, draft. Each focused. Much better than one prompt doing everything.

title: Capstone: Build a Lead-to-Student Automation Pipeline

content.explanation:
Build a complete lead-to-student pipeline in n8n with Claude as the intelligence layer. From "someone shows interest" to "onboarded student" with minimal manual intervention.

Four stages. Stage 1 (Capture): webhook receives lead form data. Stage 2 (Qualify): Claude classifies as serious/casual/spam and generates personalized response. Stage 3 (Route): switch node sends serious leads to sales path, casual to nurture path. Stage 4 (Record): every lead logged to Google Sheets.

This is production-grade. Real businesses run pipelines like this. Claude intelligence in the middle reads what the person wrote, understands intent, and crafts a personal response. Build it, test with sample submissions, and you have a working business tool.

terminology.definition: Evaluating whether a potential customer is likely to buy. Factors: budget, timeline, specificity.
A slower communication sequence for leads not yet ready to buy. Keeps them engaged.
Customer Relationship Management — tracking interactions with leads and customers. Sheets can serve as simple CRM.

codeExample.annotation: Full pipeline: webhook captures, Claude qualifies, switch routes, everything logged.

exercise.instruction: Build the pipeline in n8n. Test with 3 sample leads (serious, casual, vague). Describe your workflow and paste test results showing different routing.
steps: Add Claude HTTP Request with qualification prompt
Add Code/Set node to parse response

validation.errorMessage: Describe complete pipeline and test results with multiple lead types.
